one_line_takeaway: A compact, equation-heavy survey of adversarial ML attacks and defenses that is useful as a formalized primer, but its claimed original contributions are largely unsubstantiated -- the sole new data point is a small, incompletely specified transfer-attack table.
key_finding: As a synthesis, the paper's takeaways are: black-box gradient estimation costs O(d) queries; transfer attacks succeed most when models share feature extractors and training data; verification of an L-layer, n-neuron network is O((2n)^L) and thus intractable beyond ~10 layers; and gradient-masking defenses are brittle to adaptive attacks like BPDA, which approximates the masked gradient by an expectation over noised inputs. Its only original quantitative result is Table I: adversarial examples crafted on ResNet-18 transfer with success rates of 100.0% (self), 46.2% to VGG-16, 38.7% to DenseNet-121, and 32.1% to MobileNetV2; examples crafted on VGG-16 transfer at 41.3% to ResNet-18, 100.0% (self), 35.9% to DenseNet-121, and 28.4% to MobileNetV2 -- confirming that transferability degrades across dissimilar architectures.
core_contribution: A single-author survey/tutorial (arXiv:2502.05637, Feb 2025) that consolidates the adversarial machine learning landscape with mathematical formalism. It walks through (1) a threat-model taxonomy organized by attacker capability (white-box vs. black-box) and goal (targeted vs. untargeted), (2) evasion attacks (C&W, PGD with an adaptive step-size rule, and transfer attacks), (3) poisoning attacks (influence-function-based optimal poisoning and clean-label backdoors), (4) defenses (defensive quantization/gradient masking, interval bound propagation, adversarial training, randomized smoothing), and (5) open challenges (BPDA adaptive attacks, exponential verification complexity). The paper's front-matter claims three original contributions: an epsilon-constrained-optimization unification of attack methods, a comparative analysis of gradient-obfuscation effects in black-box attacks, and an evaluation of poisoning propagation in federated learning. In practice the substance is expository: the vast majority of methods and results are restated from cited prior work, and the only apparently-original data is one small (and incomplete) transfer-attack table.
threat_model: Standard adversarial-ML threat model, explicitly formalized by the paper. Attacker capability is split into white-box (full knowledge of architecture, parameters theta, gradients, and training data, enabling exact gradient-based crafting) and black-box (query-only access, gradients estimated via finite differences requiring O(d) queries). Attacker goals are targeted misdirection (force a specific class t under a confidence margin kappa) and untargeted deception (any misclassification), both under an L_p perturbation budget ||eta||_p <= epsilon. Attack surfaces enumerated: evasion (inference-time), poisoning (training-data), model extraction, and privacy/membership-inference. Security-critical deployment contexts cited: biometric authentication, medical diagnostics, and autonomous driving.
limitations: (1) Scope/novelty: despite claiming three original contributions, the paper is overwhelmingly a restatement of prior work; the 'evaluation of poisoning propagation in federated learning' contribution is never actually carried out -- federated learning is only mentioned in passing as a vulnerable setting, with no experiment. (2) Empirical thinness: the single empirical table (Table I) omits its dataset, attack algorithm, epsilon, and threat setting, and the header lists four source models but only two rows (ResNet-18, VGG-16) are filled in, so it is not reproducible. (3) Attribution risk for readers: several headline numbers are borrowed, not measured here -- the 99%-confidence panda-to-gibbon example is from Goodfellow et al. [18], and the '~38% reduction in attack success at 4-bit quantization' figure is cited from Guo et al. [12], not an experiment in this paper. (4) Reference hygiene: the bibliography contains duplicates (refs [3]=[4] Carlini&Wagner; [2]=[5]=[14] Madry et al.; [11]=[16] Athalye et al.), and TRADES is invoked in the conclusion without a citation or prior definition. (5) No new defense, benchmark, or theoretical result is established.

datasets_used: No dataset is stated for the paper's own Table I transfer-attack experiment (setup unspecified)
ImageNet -- referenced only as context for benchmark performance and the cited panda/gibbon example, not used in an original experiment
quantitative_results: Table I (own data) transfer ASR, source ResNet-18: ResNet-18 100.0%, VGG-16 46.2%, DenseNet-121 38.7%, MobileNetV2 32.1%
Table I (own data) transfer ASR, source VGG-16: ResNet-18 41.3%, VGG-16 100.0%, DenseNet-121 35.9%, MobileNetV2 28.4%
Cited (not own): 4-bit defensive quantization reduces attack success rates by up to 38% [Guo et al., ref 12]
Cited (not own): imperceptible noise misclassifies a panda as a gibbon with 99% confidence [Goodfellow et al., ref 18]
Analytical: black-box finite-difference gradient estimation requires O(d) queries
Analytical: exact verification of an L-layer, n-neuron-per-layer network is O((2n)^L), intractable beyond ~10 layers
baselines_compared: Evasion attacks surveyed: FGSM, PGD (with adaptive step-size variant), Carlini-Wagner (C&W), ZOO, Boundary Attack, transfer attacks, MI-FGSM, DIM, ensemble attacks
Poisoning attacks surveyed: influence-function optimal poisoning, clean-label backdoors (feature-collision, adversarial-perturbation, watermark-trigger)
Defenses surveyed: defensive quantization/gradient masking, interval bound propagation (IBP), adversarial training, randomized smoothing
Adaptive attack discussed as open challenge: BPDA
Transfer-table models: ResNet-18, VGG-16, DenseNet-121, MobileNetV2 (only ResNet-18 and VGG-16 used as source rows)
No head-to-head benchmarking of defenses is performed; methods are described, not competitively evaluated
